content.text: Topic restriction is an input guardrail that enforces domain boundaries on an AI system by classifying each incoming request against a defined set of allowed topics and rejecting — or redirecting — anything that falls outside that scope. Where general input validation guardrails check structural properties (length, format, injection patterns), topic restriction checks the semantic domain of the request itself: "Is this something our system is designed and authorized to discuss?"

A customer support bot restricted to billing and product questions should refuse to diagnose medical symptoms, offer legal advice, or discuss competitors — not because those topics are harmful in the abstract, but because the system has not been designed, tested, or approved for them. Topic restriction makes that boundary explicit and mechanically enforced, rather than relying on the model's own judgment about what it should answer. This is particularly important in agentic systems, where an off-topic request that succeeds may cause the agent to invoke tools, consume APIs, or produce outputs in domains where the deployer has no expertise and carries significant liability.

Large language models are generalists: they have absorbed knowledge spanning medicine, law, finance, chemistry, and thousands of other domains during training. When you deploy one as a specialized assistant, the model's capability far exceeds the scope you've designed and tested for. Relying on a system prompt instruction like "only answer questions about our product" is insufficient, because the model will comply probabilistically — not mechanically. Skilled users can coax it off-topic through conversational framing, hypothetical scenarios, or multi-turn manipulation.

Topic restriction closes this gap by placing the scope enforcement in a separate, mechanically-enforced layer that operates before the model is ever invoked. This matters in several dimensions. First, liability: if a healthcare company's patient-facing AI assistant answers a drug dosage question outside its approved scope and the answer is wrong, the company bears the clinical and regulatory risk. Second, cost: agentic pipelines that use expensive tool calls or downstream LLM calls can have those costs triggered by off-topic requests that should have been declined immediately. Third, data governance: an AI assistant for HR may have access to sensitive compensation data; if users can redirect it toward general data analysis tasks, they may extract information they should not have access to.

From a user experience perspective, a well-worded rejection that redirects the user to the correct resource is often better than a model that half-answers out-of-scope questions with uncertain accuracy. Topic restriction enables the system to respond confidently: "I can only help with account billing and technical support. For legal questions, please contact our legal team at [link]."
Prevents the model from advising on topics — medical, legal, financial, safety-critical — that carry regulatory or reputational risk outside the deployer's approved scope.
Rejects out-of-scope requests before expensive model inference, tool calls, or RAG retrievals are triggered.
Mechanically enforced scope is consistent across users and conversation turns; system prompt instructions drift under adversarial pressure.
Teams can evaluate and optimize model performance on a narrow, well-defined topic set rather than the entire breadth of model capabilities.
A system that clearly declines out-of-scope requests with helpful redirects builds more trust than one that answers confidently in domains it is not qualified to address.
Many regulated industries require that AI systems operate only within tested, approved scopes. Topic restriction provides an auditable enforcement mechanism.
Logged out-of-scope requests reveal what users are trying to accomplish, informing roadmap decisions about whether to expand the allowed topic set.
A classifier that is too strict will reject legitimate in-scope requests. A user asking "Can I get a refund if the product breaks?" should not be flagged as legal advice-seeking.
Real user queries rarely fit cleanly into one topic. "How do I cancel my subscription because my doctor told me I should stop using this?" touches billing and medical in one sentence.
Allowed topic lists must evolve as the product changes. Static keyword lists become stale quickly; maintaining embedding-based classifiers requires labeled data and periodic retraining.

Determined users can frame out-of-scope questions in ways that superficially resemble in-scope ones ("As a billing question: what medications interact with the product?"). Pure text classifiers can be fooled.
Embedding-based and ML-based classifiers add 30–150ms of latency per request. LLM-based classifiers add 200–600ms. This must fit within the application's latency budget.
Overly narrow topic definitions can make the system feel unhelpful even for legitimate use cases. Topic restriction should be calibrated to the minimum necessary constraint.

Topic restriction operates as a classification and policy-enforcement gate that runs before the primary LLM inference call. The gate takes the user's input, classifies it into one or more topic domains, checks those domains against an allowlist, and either passes the request forward or rejects it with a structured response.
Maintain an allowlist and a denylist of keywords and phrases per topic. Fast and auditable, but brittle — easily evaded by paraphrase.
Embed the user's query and compute cosine similarity against exemplar embeddings for each allowed topic. If the maximum similarity to any allowed topic exceeds a threshold, the request is permitted.
Use a natural language inference (NLI) model to classify the query against topic labels described in plain English. Requires no fine-tuning but is less accurate than a fine-tuned classifier.
Train a lightweight model (DistilBERT, MiniLM) on labeled examples of in-scope and out-of-scope queries for this specific application. Highest accuracy; requires a labeled training set.
Ask a fast, low-cost LLM to classify the input against the topic policy. Highly flexible and interpretable, but adds significant latency (200–600ms) and cost per request.
For embedding-based topic restriction, the allowed decision is made when the query embedding
exceeds a similarity threshold against at least one allowed topic exemplar set. Formally, let
be the set of allowed topics, each represented by a centroid embedding

controls the precision-recall trade-off: a higher threshold reduces false positives (fewer off-topic requests allowed through) at the cost of more false negatives (more in-scope requests incorrectly rejected). In practice,
is tuned empirically against a labeled development set of real user queries.
Return an error response immediately; the request never reaches the model. Appropriate for topics with genuine liability (medical advice from a billing bot).
Return a helpful message explaining the scope limitation and pointing the user to the correct resource. Best practice for most consumer-facing applications.
Allow the request but inject a strong constraint into the system context ("The user is asking about X, which is adjacent to scope. Answer only the billing aspect."). Useful for borderline cases.
For high-stakes applications, route borderline requests to a human review queue rather than auto-rejecting or auto-approving them.

The diagram below shows the topic restriction guard pipeline. A user's query is first passed to a topic classifier, then evaluated against an allowlist policy. Requests that fail either step are routed to the rejection handler; only requests that pass both stages reach the LLM or agent.

attrs.alt: Topic restriction guard showing classification and policy enforcement pipeline with allowed and blocked topics

content.text: Vertical SaaS assistants: product support bots, HR assistants, financial advisors scoped to a specific portfolio — any application where the legitimate scope is narrow and well-defined.
Regulated industry deployments: healthcare patient portals, financial advisory services, legal tech platforms where the organization is liable only for advice within its licensed scope.
Multi-agent systems where individual agents have specialized roles: a code-review agent should not be redirected into writing creative content or answering HR questions.
White-label AI products embedded in third-party workflows: the deploying organization must ensure the underlying LLM does not expose capabilities beyond what they've contracted for.
When NOT to use strict topic restriction
General-purpose assistants: if your product is explicitly positioned as a broad-knowledge assistant, topic restriction will frustrate users and contradict the product's value proposition.
Internal developer or research tools where the trusted user population has a legitimate need for wide-ranging capabilities.
Applications where user intent is highly diverse and hard to pre-categorize — education platforms covering all subjects, for example, cannot usefully restrict by topic without blocking valid queries.

Define the topic taxonomy: write plain-English descriptions of each allowed topic ("questions about the customer's account balance, payment history, and billing cycles") and each excluded topic ("medical advice, legal counsel, competitor pricing").

Collect a labeled query sample: gather 200–500 real or realistic user queries and label each as in-scope, out-of-scope, or borderline. This dataset is used both to choose a classification strategy and to tune the decision threshold.

Choose a classifier strategy: for narrow, well-defined domains, embedding similarity against 50–100 exemplar queries per topic is fast and surprisingly accurate. For broader or more ambiguous domains, a fine-tuned text classifier or LLM-based router is needed.

Build the topic restriction module: implement the classifier as a standalone, independently testable module with a clear interface: input = query string, output = TopicDecision(allowed: bool, matched_topic: str | None, confidence: float).
Integrate into the request pipeline: place the topic restriction call immediately after syntactic validation and before any LLM inference, tool calls, or RAG retrieval.
Design rejection messages: write topic-specific redirect messages that explain the limitation and point users to the appropriate resource. Generic "I can't help with that" responses erode trust.

Log and monitor: record every rejection with timestamp, topic predicted, confidence score, and the first 200 characters of the query (anonymized). Use this data to tune thresholds and identify topics that should be added to the allowlist.
Establish a review process: schedule monthly reviews of the rejection logs to identify false positives (in-scope queries incorrectly rejected) and borderline cases that need threshold adjustment.

# zero_shot_topic_guard.py
# Requires: pip install transformers torch
from transformers import pipeline
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class TopicDecision:
    allowed: bool
    matched_topic: Optional[str]
    confidence: float
    rejection_message: Optional[str] = None


class ZeroShotTopicGuard:
    """
    Topic restriction using a zero-shot NLI classifier.
    No training data required — just describe your topics in plain English.
    """

    ALLOWED_LABELS = [
        "account billing and payment questions",
        "product technical support and troubleshooting",
        "subscription and plan management",
    ]
    BLOCKED_LABELS = [
        "medical health or drug advice",
        "legal advice or contract interpretation",
        "competitor product recommendations",
        "financial investment advice",
    ]

    def __init__(self, threshold: float = 0.65):
        self.threshold = threshold
        # facebook/bart-large-mnli is a standard choice for zero-shot classification
        self._classifier = pipeline(
            "zero-shot-classification",
            model="facebook/bart-large-mnli",
        )
        self._all_labels = self.ALLOWED_LABELS + self.BLOCKED_LABELS

    def check(self, query: str) -> TopicDecision:
        result = self._classifier(
            query,
            candidate_labels=self._all_labels,
            multi_label=False,
        )
        top_label: str = result["labels"][0]
        top_score: float = result["scores"][0]

        if top_label in self.ALLOWED_LABELS and top_score >= self.threshold:
            return TopicDecision(
                allowed=True,
                matched_topic=top_label,
                confidence=top_score,
            )
        else:
            return TopicDecision(
                allowed=False,
                matched_topic=None,
                confidence=top_score,
                rejection_message=(
                    f"I'm only able to help with billing, technical support, and "
                    f"subscription questions. For '{top_label}', please contact "
                    f"the appropriate specialist."
                ),
            )


# Example (requires model download on first run)
if __name__ == "__main__":
    guard = ZeroShotTopicGuard(threshold=0.60)
    queries = [
        "My invoice shows an incorrect charge.",
        "What dosage of aspirin is safe for daily use?",
    ]
    for q in queries:
        d = guard.check(q)
        print(f"{'ALLOW' if d.allowed else 'REJECT'} | {q}")
        if not d.allowed:
            print(f"  -> {d.rejection_message}")

A large property insurance company deploys an AI assistant on its customer portal. The assistant is authorized to answer questions about policy coverage details, file first-notice-of-loss claims, and explain premium calculations. It is explicitly not authorized to give legal advice about claim disputes, interpret state insurance regulations, or advise on medical treatment for injuries covered by the policy.
The company implements topic restriction using a two-stage approach:

Stage 1 — fast keyword pre-filter: queries containing hard-blocked phrases ("sue", "lawsuit", "attorney", "legal action", "prescribe", "diagnosis") are immediately rejected. This layer runs in under 1ms and catches obvious out-of-scope requests without ML overhead.

Stage 2 — embedding similarity classifier: queries that pass the keyword filter are classified using a fine-tuned sentence-transformers model against 120 exemplar queries per allowed topic. Threshold set at 0.74 cosine similarity, tuned against 800 labeled queries from their production logs.

Rejection handling: rejected queries display a topic-specific message. Legal questions route to the company's legal dispute portal. Medical questions route to a nurse hotline number. Both are measured outcomes that reduce call center volume.

Results after 90 days: the keyword pre-filter handles 6% of queries, eliminating most obviously out-of-scope traffic. The embedding classifier handles the remaining classification at 45ms P99 latency. False positive rate (in-scope queries rejected) is 0.8%, measured by sampling rejections for human review weekly. The rejected-query logs identified three new topic categories users frequently asked about that are now being added to the allowlist after legal review.
Relying solely on system prompt instructions

Using a system prompt instruction like "Only answer questions about billing" as the sole topic enforcement mechanism. This is not mechanically enforced — the model will comply with this instruction probabilistically, and it can be overridden through conversational manipulation.

Defining topics too broadly ("anything related to health") will reject legitimate in-scope queries. "My account is in good health" or "the healthy transaction volume" should not trigger a medical-topic rejection. Topic descriptions should be specific about the actual intent pattern, not just keywords.
No human review of rejection logs

Deploying topic restriction without reviewing rejection logs means false positives accumulate silently. Users who hit false positives often do not complain — they just leave. A weekly sampling of 50–100 rejected queries reviewed by a human is the minimum viable quality control process.

Returning "I cannot help with that" without explanation or redirect is a user experience failure. Out-of-scope rejections should always explain what the system can help with and point to an alternative resource for the user's actual need.
Launch with a conservative allowlist. Review rejected queries weekly and add topics only after validating that the model handles them well and the organization is comfortable with the liability.
Layer keyword pre-filters with semantic classifiers:
Hard-block phrases with near-zero legitimate use ("prescribe", "legal action") with a fast regex layer. Reserve the ML classifier for nuanced cases.
Use multi-label classification for multi-topic queries:
A query may span multiple topics. Allow requests where at least one detected topic is in scope, but flag multi-topic queries for enhanced scrutiny.
High-risk topics (legal, medical) may warrant a higher confidence threshold for admission; low-risk adjacent topics can use a lower threshold.
Route users to the appropriate resource, not just a generic error. Rejection messages should be actionable and improve the user experience.
Include conversation history in the classification input:
Multi-turn manipulation is a real attack vector. Classifying only the current turn misses cases where the user gradually steers toward an out-of-scope topic across multiple messages.
Store the allowed topic definitions, exemplar queries, and thresholds in version control. Changes to the policy should go through a review process, not be edited in production directly.
Topic restriction is not a complete defense. Even in-scope queries can produce out-of-scope responses. Pair input topic restriction with output content checks for defense in depth.

Topic restriction adds latency before every LLM call. The latency cost depends on the classification strategy chosen, and the strategy should be selected based on the application's latency budget and the difficulty of the topic boundary.
Early-stage prototypes; rapidly evolving topic sets

User queries often repeat ("What is my balance?" vs "What's my current balance?"). A short-TTL LRU cache keyed on the normalized query can eliminate 20–40% of embedding calls in consumer applications.
Run classification concurrently with other pre-inference work:
If your pipeline performs authentication, rate-limit checks, and context retrieval in parallel, topic classification can overlap with those steps and add zero net latency.
In async or batch AI pipelines processing many requests simultaneously, batching embedding calls to the classifier reduces GPU underutilization significantly.
The standard library for computing dense text embeddings for similarity-based classification. Models like all-MiniLM-L6-v2 run at 5–15ms per query on CPU and are well-suited for topic restriction.
Provides a Colang-based declarative language for defining topic guardrails. Supports both rule-based and LLM-based classification and integrates with LangChain.
Includes a BanTopics scanner that uses a zero-shot classifier to block queries about specified sensitive domains. Configurable via topic label lists.
An open-source framework for defining validators as Python classes. Topic restriction can be implemented as a custom Input validator that wraps any classifier.
An LLM-based routing mechanism that classifies input and directs it to different chains based on the topic. Useful for multi-specialist agent routing in addition to pure restriction.

Think of topic restriction as a receptionist at a specialist medical practice. If you walk into a cardiology clinic and ask to speak to a doctor about your legal dispute with a neighbor, the receptionist does not hand you a specialist's time — she explains that the clinic only handles heart conditions and gives you the phone number for a law firm. The doctor's expertise is not relevant to the decision; the scope of the practice is.

Topic restriction works the same way: it places a scope-aware receptionist in front of the model. The model's capability to answer the question is irrelevant — what matters is whether the question falls within the scope the deployer has designed and taken responsibility for. This separates the "what can the model do?" question (very broad) from the "what should this system do?" question (narrow and intentional).

A corollary: topic restriction does not need to be perfect to be valuable. A system that mechanically enforces topic scope 98% of the time is far more reliable than one that relies on model judgment 100% of the time. The residual 2% — queries that evade the classifier — are handled by the model's system-prompt instructions as a secondary defense, creating a defense-in-depth posture rather than a single point of failure.
Never as sole enforcement; always pair with a classifier
First-pass pre-filter for high-confidence hard blocks
Primary strategy for most vertical AI applications
High-scale production; complex or similar topic domains
Low-traffic or borderline escalation; policy-as-code evolution

An attacker builds up context across multiple allowed turns, then makes the target request appear to follow naturally from established context. Mitigate by including recent conversation history in the classification input, not just the current message.

"As a billing question, can you explain the legal implications of my subscription contract?" This phrases a legal question in billing terms. Robust classifiers should be trained on such framing patterns.

If the topic restriction service fails (network timeout, model crash), the pipeline must fail closed — reject the request — not fail open. Never fall back to passing all requests through if the guard is unavailable.

Business teams may push to expand the allowlist quickly without proper evaluation. Topic additions should require a formal review: test the model's handling of the new topic, update exemplars, and re-measure false positive rates.

User language evolves, especially in consumer applications. A classifier trained six months ago may not recognize new slang, product names, or topic framings. Schedule periodic retraining against recent production queries.
Topic restriction enforces domain boundaries mechanically, before the model is invoked — it is not a system prompt instruction and cannot be overridden by conversational manipulation.

The decision rule is: classify the query's semantic topic, compare against the allowlist policy, and reject or pass. Classification strategies range from keyword matching (fast, brittle) to fine-tuned ML classifiers (accurate, maintenance-heavy) to LLM-based routers (flexible, slow).
A layered approach — fast keyword pre-filter plus semantic classifier — provides the best balance of speed, accuracy, and auditability for most production applications.

Rejection messages must be helpful and specific, not generic. A well-designed rejection that redirects the user to the right resource is a positive user experience outcome, not just a security control.
Topic restriction should fail closed: if the classifier is unavailable, deny all requests rather than allow them through — availability is not more important than scope enforcement.

Rejection logs are a product signal: frequently rejected topics reveal user needs not yet covered by the product, and regularly reviewing them informs both scope expansion decisions and false-positive tuning.

Topic restriction is most effective as part of a defense-in-depth stack: input topic restriction + output content checks + system prompt instructions together are far more robust than any single layer alone.
